title: Cybersecurity
description: Go from security fundamentals through networking, web (OWASP), crypto, and defense to hands-on CTFs and labs.

sections.title: Security Fundamentals
sections.description: Build the core vocabulary and mental models that every security discipline is built on.

topics.title: The CIA Triad and Core Concepts
topics.description: Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability are the three goals every security control ultimately serves. This topic covers those pillars plus supporting ideas like non-repudiation and accountability.
topics.why: Almost every security decision is a trade-off between these three goals. Naming which one you are protecting turns vague 'be more secure' requests into concrete, testable requirements.
learn: Define confidentiality, integrity, and availability with a real-world example of each
Map common controls (encryption, hashing, backups) to the pillar they protect
Learn how non-repudiation and authenticity extend the basic triad
Practice classifying a breach headline by which pillar failed
criteria: You can explain the CIA triad and give a concrete example for each pillar
You can classify a given control by which security goal it supports
You can describe non-repudiation in one sentence

topics.title: Threats, Vulnerabilities, and Risk
topics.description: The relationship between a threat (who or what could cause harm), a vulnerability (the weakness), and risk (likelihood times impact). Includes the basics of threat modeling.
topics.why: Security work is prioritization. Understanding risk lets you focus limited time on the weaknesses that actually matter instead of chasing every theoretical flaw.
learn: Distinguish threat, threat actor, vulnerability, exploit, and risk with definitions
Learn the risk equation (likelihood x impact) and how it drives prioritization
Walk through a simple STRIDE threat model for a small app
List common threat actor types and their typical motivations
criteria: You can define threat, vulnerability, and risk and explain how they relate
You can produce a basic threat model listing assets, threats, and mitigations
You can rank two risks by likelihood and impact and justify the order

topics.title: Authentication and Authorization
topics.description: Authentication proves who you are; authorization decides what you may do. Covers factors, sessions, tokens, and least-privilege access models.
topics.why: Broken auth is one of the most common and damaging classes of vulnerability. Getting identity and access right is the foundation of protecting every other resource.
learn: Distinguish authentication from authorization with a clear example
Learn the three authentication factors and what multi-factor auth adds
Compare role-based and attribute-based access control models
Understand the principle of least privilege and why it limits blast radius

resources.label: Cloudflare — What is identity and access management?

criteria: You can explain the difference between authentication and authorization
You can list the authentication factors and describe an MFA setup
You can apply least privilege to a sample set of user roles

topics.title: The Attacker Mindset and Ethics
topics.description: Learning to think like an adversary: enumerating attack surface, chaining small flaws, and questioning assumptions, all within legal and ethical boundaries.
topics.why: You cannot defend what you cannot attack in your head first. An adversarial mindset, paired with strict ethics and authorization, is what separates a security professional from a liability.
learn: Learn the phases of an attack (recon, exploitation, persistence, exfiltration)
Study why explicit authorization and scope are legally required before testing
Practice enumerating the attack surface of a familiar application
Read a responsible-disclosure policy and note what it does and does not permit
criteria: You can outline the phases of a typical attack
You can explain why written authorization and scope matter legally
You can enumerate the attack surface of a simple system

sections.title: Networking Security
sections.description: Understand how networks move data and how attackers and defenders operate at each layer.

topics.title: Networking Fundamentals
topics.description: The TCP/IP and OSI models, IP addressing, packets, and how data actually travels between two machines on the internet.
topics.why: Nearly every attack and defense happens over a network. Without a solid grasp of how packets route and protocols layer, tools like Wireshark and Nmap are just noise.
learn: Learn the OSI and TCP/IP layer models and what each layer does
Understand IP addresses, subnets, and the difference between TCP and UDP
Trace what happens end to end when you load a web page
Read a captured packet and identify its source, destination, and protocol

resources.label: Cloudflare — How does the internet work?

criteria: You can describe the OSI layers and give an example protocol at each
You can explain the difference between TCP and UDP
You can walk through the steps of loading a web page over the network

topics.title: Common Protocols and Ports
topics.description: The everyday protocols (HTTP/S, DNS, SSH, SMTP, DHCP) and their default ports, plus how insecure protocols leak data.
topics.why: Recognizing services by port and protocol lets you read scan output instantly and spot when something insecure (like plaintext FTP or Telnet) is exposed.
learn: Memorize the default ports for HTTP, HTTPS, DNS, SSH, and SMTP
Compare a plaintext protocol with its encrypted equivalent (HTTP vs HTTPS)
Learn how DNS resolution works and why it is a common attack target
Run a port scan on a machine you own and interpret the open services
criteria: You can name the default ports for the major protocols
You can explain how DNS turns a name into an IP address
You can interpret the results of a basic port scan

topics.title: Firewalls, VPNs, and Network Defense
topics.description: The controls that segment and protect networks: firewalls, VPNs, network segmentation, and intrusion detection systems.
topics.why: These are the front-line defenses in every organization. Knowing how they filter, tunnel, and alert helps you both configure them and understand what an attacker must bypass.
learn: Learn how stateful firewalls filter traffic by rules
Understand what a VPN encrypts and the threats it does and does not stop
Compare network segmentation and a flat network's blast radius
Learn the difference between an IDS and an IPS
criteria: You can explain how a stateful firewall decides to allow or block traffic
You can describe what a VPN protects and its limitations
You can distinguish an IDS from an IPS

topics.title: Network Attacks and Analysis
topics.description: Common network-layer attacks such as sniffing, man-in-the-middle, ARP spoofing, and denial of service, plus how to analyze traffic to spot them.
topics.why: Recognizing malicious traffic patterns is core to both offense and defense. Packet analysis skills turn an invisible network into readable evidence.
learn: Learn how sniffing and man-in-the-middle attacks intercept traffic
Understand ARP spoofing and why local networks are vulnerable to it
Study how DoS and DDoS attacks exhaust resources
Capture and inspect live traffic with Wireshark on a network you own

resources.label: Cloudflare — What is a DDoS attack?

criteria: You can explain how a man-in-the-middle attack works
You can describe the difference between DoS and DDoS
You captured traffic and identified protocols in Wireshark

sections.title: Systems and Tooling
sections.description: Get comfortable on the command line and with the operating systems where security work happens.

topics.title: Linux Fundamentals
topics.description: Navigating the filesystem, managing users and permissions, and understanding processes on Linux, the dominant platform for security tooling.
topics.why: Most security tools, servers, and CTF targets run Linux. Fluency with the shell and permission model is the single highest-leverage skill for a security practitioner.
learn: Navigate the filesystem and manage files from the shell
Understand users, groups, and the read/write/execute permission model
Inspect running processes and services
Read and search log files in /var/log
criteria: You can navigate and manipulate files entirely from the shell
You can read and modify Linux file permissions
You completed the early levels of OverTheWire Bandit

topics.title: Command-Line Security Tooling
topics.description: The workhorse command-line tools for reconnaissance and analysis, including Nmap, Netcat, curl, and grep-based log triage.
topics.why: These tools are the daily drivers of security work. Chaining them fluently on the command line is far faster than any point-and-click alternative.
learn: Run and interpret a basic Nmap host and port scan
Use curl to inspect HTTP requests and response headers
Practice filtering text with grep, awk, and pipes
Learn what Netcat can do for simple connections and listeners
criteria: You can run an Nmap scan and read its output
You can inspect HTTP headers with curl
You can filter a log file down to relevant lines using pipes

topics.title: System Hardening
topics.description: Reducing a system's attack surface: patching, disabling unused services, secure configuration, and applying baselines.
topics.why: A default installation is rarely secure. Hardening is one of the most cost-effective defensive activities, closing doors before an attacker ever finds them.
learn: Learn why patching and update management matter and how they fail
Disable or remove unnecessary services and default accounts
Apply a configuration baseline or benchmark to a system
Enable and review host-level logging
criteria: You can explain why unused services should be disabled
You applied a hardening baseline to a test system
You can describe a safe patch-management process

sections.title: Cryptography Basics
sections.description: Learn the building blocks that make confidentiality, integrity, and trust possible on the internet.

topics.title: Symmetric and Asymmetric Encryption
topics.description: The two families of encryption: shared-key (symmetric) and public/private key (asymmetric), and when each is used.
topics.why: Encryption underpins nearly all secure communication. Knowing which scheme solves which problem lets you reason about protocols like TLS instead of treating them as magic.
learn: Learn how symmetric ciphers use one shared key
Understand how public/private key pairs enable encryption and signatures
See how the two are combined in practice (key exchange plus bulk encryption)
Learn why you should never roll your own crypto

resources.label: Cloudflare — How does public key encryption work?

criteria: You can explain the difference between symmetric and asymmetric encryption
You can describe when each type is appropriate
You can explain why implementing your own cipher is risky

topics.title: Hashing and Integrity
topics.description: One-way hash functions, message authentication codes, and how they verify integrity and protect stored passwords.
topics.why: Hashing is how systems verify that data has not changed and store passwords without keeping the plaintext. Confusing hashing with encryption is a classic and dangerous mistake.
learn: Learn what makes a cryptographic hash one-way and collision-resistant
Understand why passwords are salted and hashed, not encrypted
Learn how HMACs verify both integrity and authenticity
Compare a fast hash to a purpose-built password hash like bcrypt
criteria: You can explain how hashing differs from encryption
You can describe why salting passwords defeats precomputed attacks
You can explain what an HMAC provides

topics.title: TLS, Certificates, and PKI
topics.description: How TLS secures web traffic, and how certificates and public key infrastructure establish trust between strangers on the internet.
topics.why: TLS is the padlock behind HTTPS and countless other protocols. Understanding certificates and chains of trust lets you diagnose the security warnings users actually see.
learn: Learn what the TLS handshake accomplishes at a high level
Understand how certificates bind an identity to a public key
Trace a certificate chain from a leaf up to a trusted root
Inspect a real site's certificate in your browser

resources.label: Cloudflare — What is a TLS handshake?

criteria: You can summarize what the TLS handshake establishes
You can explain how certificate chains create trust
You inspected a live certificate and read its issuer and validity

topics.title: Practical Cryptography Pitfalls
topics.description: The common ways real systems misuse otherwise-sound cryptography: weak randomness, hardcoded keys, deprecated algorithms, and poor key management.
topics.why: In practice, crypto rarely fails because the math is broken; it fails because it is used incorrectly. Spotting these misuses is a high-value review skill.
learn: Learn why predictable randomness breaks security guarantees
Identify deprecated algorithms (MD5, SHA-1, DES) and their replacements
Understand the risks of hardcoded secrets and how to manage keys
Review a code sample for a cryptographic misuse
criteria: You can name deprecated hash and cipher algorithms and their replacements
You can explain why secrets should not be hardcoded
You identified a crypto misuse in a sample

sections.title: Web Security (OWASP)
sections.description: Learn how web applications are attacked and hardened, using the OWASP body of knowledge.

topics.title: How the Web Works
topics.description: The HTTP request/response cycle, cookies and sessions, the same-origin policy, and the browser security model.
topics.why: You cannot secure what you do not understand. A precise model of HTTP and the browser is the prerequisite for every web vulnerability class that follows.
learn: Trace an HTTP request and response including methods, headers, and status codes
Learn how cookies maintain sessions across stateless requests
Understand the same-origin policy and why it exists
Use browser developer tools to inspect requests and cookies
criteria: You can describe the HTTP request/response cycle
You can explain how cookies enable sessions
You can state what the same-origin policy protects against

topics.title: The OWASP Top 10
topics.description: The industry-standard list of the most critical web application security risks, from broken access control to security misconfiguration.
topics.why: The OWASP Top 10 is the shared language of web security. Knowing each category gives you a checklist for reviewing, testing, and prioritizing web risks.
learn: Read through all ten categories and summarize each in a sentence
Map each category to a concrete example vulnerability
Identify which categories relate to access control versus injection
Pick one category to study in depth via the linked labs
criteria: You can name and briefly describe each OWASP Top 10 category
You can give a concrete example for at least five categories
You completed at least one lab in a chosen category

topics.title: Injection and Cross-Site Scripting
topics.description: Injection flaws (SQL injection, command injection) and cross-site scripting (XSS), where untrusted input is executed as code or queries.
topics.why: Injection and XSS remain among the most exploited web flaws. Understanding how untrusted input crosses a trust boundary is central to writing and reviewing secure code.
learn: Learn how SQL injection manipulates a query with untrusted input
Understand the difference between reflected, stored, and DOM-based XSS
Learn how parameterized queries and output encoding stop these attacks
Exploit a deliberately vulnerable lab to see the flaw first-hand
criteria: You can explain how SQL injection works and how to prevent it
You can distinguish the three main types of XSS
You exploited an injection or XSS lab successfully

topics.title: Authentication and Access Control on the Web
topics.description: Web-specific auth pitfalls: broken access control, insecure session handling, CSRF, and insecure direct object references.
topics.why: Access control failures top the OWASP list because they are easy to introduce and devastating to exploit. These bugs let attackers act as other users or admins.
learn: Learn how broken access control lets users reach data they should not
Understand insecure direct object references (IDOR) via manipulated IDs
Learn how CSRF forces authenticated actions and how tokens stop it
Test access control by changing identifiers in a lab environment
criteria: You can explain what broken access control looks like in practice
You can describe an IDOR and how to prevent it
You can explain how CSRF tokens defend against forged requests

topics.title: Web Testing with an Intercepting Proxy
topics.description: Using an intercepting proxy such as Burp Suite to observe, modify, and replay web traffic while testing an application.
topics.why: An intercepting proxy is the essential tool of web security testing. It turns the browser into an editable window on every request, revealing what the app really does.
learn: Configure your browser to route traffic through an intercepting proxy
Intercept, edit, and forward a live request
Replay and tamper with requests to probe validation
Work through structured proxy exercises in a training lab
criteria: You configured a proxy and intercepted live traffic
You modified a request and observed the changed response
You completed proxy-based lab exercises

sections.title: Defensive Practice
sections.description: Apply security knowledge to detect, respond to, and reduce risk in real environments.

topics.title: Defense in Depth
topics.description: Layering independent controls so that no single failure leads to compromise, spanning network, host, application, and human layers.
topics.why: Every control eventually fails. Defense in depth ensures a breach in one layer meets another barrier, dramatically raising the cost of a successful attack.
learn: Learn the layered-control model and give an example at each layer
Understand how preventive, detective, and corrective controls differ
Map controls you already know onto a defense-in-depth diagram
Identify a single point of failure in a sample architecture
criteria: You can explain defense in depth with layered examples
You can classify controls as preventive, detective, or corrective
You identified a single point of failure in an architecture

topics.title: Vulnerability Management
topics.description: The cycle of discovering, prioritizing, remediating, and verifying vulnerabilities, including scanning and severity scoring.
topics.why: Organizations face more vulnerabilities than they can ever fix at once. A repeatable management process is what keeps the most dangerous issues from lingering.
learn: Learn the vulnerability management lifecycle end to end
Understand how CVSS scores communicate severity
Run a vulnerability scan against a lab target and read the report
Prioritize a list of findings using severity and exposure
criteria: You can describe the vulnerability management lifecycle
You can interpret a CVSS severity score
You ran a scan and prioritized its findings

topics.title: Logging, Monitoring, and Detection
topics.description: Collecting and analyzing logs, understanding SIEM concepts, and writing detections that turn raw events into actionable alerts.
topics.why: You cannot respond to what you cannot see. Good logging and detection shrink the time between compromise and discovery, which is the metric attackers most want to grow.
learn: Learn what events are worth logging and why centralization matters
Understand the role of a SIEM in correlating events
Read authentication and system logs to spot anomalies
Write a simple detection rule for a suspicious pattern
criteria: You can explain what a SIEM does
You can identify a suspicious event in a log sample
You wrote a basic detection rule for a defined pattern

topics.title: Incident Response Basics
topics.description: The structured process for handling security incidents: preparation, identification, containment, eradication, recovery, and lessons learned.
topics.why: How an organization reacts in the first hours of a breach often matters more than the breach itself. A rehearsed process prevents panic-driven mistakes that make things worse.
learn: Learn the phases of the incident response lifecycle
Understand the difference between containment and eradication
Study why preparation and playbooks decide outcomes under pressure
Walk through a tabletop scenario for a simulated incident
criteria: You can list the incident response phases in order
You can explain the difference between containment and eradication
You walked through a tabletop incident scenario

sections.title: CTFs, Labs, and Growth
sections.description: Cement your skills through deliberate hands-on practice and plug into the security community.

topics.title: Capture the Flag Fundamentals
topics.description: How CTF competitions work, the common categories (web, crypto, forensics, reversing, pwn), and how to approach a challenge methodically.
topics.why: CTFs compress real skills into safe, legal challenges with instant feedback. They are the fastest way to turn theory into applied problem-solving.
learn: Learn the standard CTF categories and what each tests
Practice a repeatable approach: enumerate, hypothesize, test, iterate
Solve a few beginner web and crypto challenges
Read a write-up to learn techniques you missed
criteria: You can describe the main CTF categories
You solved at least three beginner CTF challenges
You read a write-up and explained one new technique

topics.title: Guided Hands-On Labs
topics.description: Structured learning platforms that walk you through attacking and defending realistic machines with guidance and hints.
topics.why: Guided labs bridge the gap between reading and free-form CTFs. They build muscle memory on real tools against real targets in a legal, sandboxed setting.
learn: Complete a beginner-friendly guided learning path
Reproduce an attack you read about in a controlled lab
Take notes on each tool and command as you use it
Redo a lab from memory without the hints
criteria: You completed a full guided learning path
You reproduced an attack in a controlled lab
You redid a lab without relying on hints

topics.title: Building a Home Lab
topics.description: Setting up your own isolated environment with virtual machines and a security-focused distribution to practice safely and offline.
topics.why: A personal lab removes all excuses and legal risk. Owning both attacker and target machines lets you experiment freely and learn how tools behave without guardrails.
learn: Set up a virtualization tool and create isolated virtual machines
Install a security-focused distribution as your attack box
Deploy a deliberately vulnerable target VM to practice against
Configure a host-only network so the lab stays isolated
criteria: You built an isolated lab with an attacker and target VM
You confirmed the lab network cannot reach the internet
You ran an end-to-end attack in your own lab

topics.title: Career Paths and Community
topics.description: Mapping the field's roles (red team, blue team, GRC, appsec), relevant certifications, and how to plug into the security community.
topics.why: Cybersecurity is broad, and knowing the landscape helps you steer your learning toward a role you actually enjoy while building a network that surfaces opportunities.
learn: Compare offensive, defensive, and governance-focused career tracks
Research entry-level certifications relevant to your target path
Build a portfolio of write-ups and completed labs
Follow reputable practitioners and join a community to stay current
criteria: You can describe the main cybersecurity career tracks
You identified a certification aligned with your goals
You started a portfolio of write-ups or completed labs
